evidence_text: SWE-bench measures whether a coding agent can submit a patch for a real GitHub issue that passes the issue’s unit tests.
source_excerpt: The source says SWE-bench measures "How well a coding agent can submit a patch for a real-world GitHub issue that passes the unit tests for that issue."
note: This evidence states the benchmark’s stated target, not whether that target matches broader software engineering work.
caveat: This evidence states the benchmark’s stated target, not whether that target matches broader software engineering work.

evidence_text: SWE-bench Verified has 500 problems and all are in Python.
source_excerpt: The source states: "It has 500 problems, all in Python."
note: This is a dataset composition fact about SWE-bench Verified.
caveat: This is a dataset composition fact about SWE-bench Verified.

evidence_text: More than 40% of SWE-bench Verified issues come from the Django repository, and web applications are entirely missing.
source_excerpt: The source says: "Over 40% are issues from the Django source repository; the rest are libraries. Web applications are entirely missing."
note: This introduces a coverage limitation in repository mix and application type.
caveat: This introduces a coverage limitation in repository mix and application type.

evidence_text: SWE-bench Verified solutions are small, with mean solution size 11 lines of code and median solution size 4 lines.
source_excerpt: The source states: "The mean lines of code per solution are 11, and median lines of code are 4."
note: This is a size statistic for benchmark solutions, not a quality judgment.
caveat: This is a size statistic for benchmark solutions, not a quality judgment.

evidence_text: Amazon found that more than 77.6% of SWE-bench Verified solutions touch only one function.
source_excerpt: The source says: "Amazon found that over 77.6% of the solutions touch only one function."
note: This describes the locality of benchmark fixes.
caveat: This describes the locality of benchmark fixes.

evidence_text: Because SWE-bench Verified issues are from 2023 and earlier, the author says the data was almost certainly in training sets and improvements are hard to separate from memorization.
source_excerpt: The source states: "All the issues are from 2023 and earlier. This data was almost certainly in the training sets. Thus it’s hard to tell how much of the improvements are due to memorisation."
note: This is a contamination caveat that limits attribution of benchmark gains to general problem-solving rather than training exposure.
caveat: This is a contamination caveat that limits attribution of benchmark gains to general problem-solving rather than training exposure.

evidence_text: SWE-bench Pro contains 1,865 problems from 41 repositories.
source_excerpt: The source says: "1865 problems, from 41 repositories."
note: This is a scale and source-count fact for SWE-bench Pro.
caveat: This is a scale and source-count fact for SWE-bench Pro.

evidence_text: SWE-bench Pro uses Python, Go, JavaScript, and TypeScript repositories, but the source says their distribution is not uniform and does not disclose the exact mix.
source_excerpt: The source states: "The repositories are Python, Go, JS and TS—we don’t know the distribution, except that they are 'not uniform'."
note: This preserves an explicit uncertainty about language distribution.
caveat: This preserves an explicit uncertainty about language distribution.

evidence_text: SWE-bench Pro solutions have mean size 107 lines of code, median size 55 lines, and span an average of 4 files.
source_excerpt: The source says: "The solutions have a mean of 107 lines of code, and a median of 55 lines of code, and usually span an average of 4 files."
note: This is a complexity and spread statistic for SWE-bench Pro tasks.
caveat: This is a complexity and spread statistic for SWE-bench Pro tasks.

evidence_text: SWE-bench Pro uses human-rewritten problems grounded in issues, commits, and pull requests to reduce missing information.
source_excerpt: The source states: "They actually got humans to rewrite problems based on issues, commits and PRs to ensure there’s no missing information."
note: This describes benchmark construction method, not outcome quality.
caveat: This describes benchmark construction method, not outcome quality.

evidence_text: SWE-bench Pro provides dockerized environments with dependencies already installed, so it does not test repository setup ability.
source_excerpt: The source says: "They also have dockerized environments set up with all the dependencies installed and configured, so the benchmark explicitly does not test if your agent can setup your repository."
note: This is a scope boundary on what the benchmark excludes.
caveat: This is a scope boundary on what the benchmark excludes.

evidence_text: The source defines a 25% SWE-bench Pro score as 25% of well-defined benchmark issues producing solutions whose unit tests pass.
source_excerpt: The source says: "So when we say that an agent scores 25% in SWE-bench Pro, we are saying: 'In a problem set of well-defined issues with pointed requirements and (the occasional) specification of code interfaces, 25% of the solutions from the agent get the respective problem’s unit test cases to pass'."
note: This keeps the benchmark interpretation tied to well-defined issues and unit-test validation only.
caveat: This keeps the benchmark interpretation tied to well-defined issues and unit-test validation only.

evidence_text: The source says SWE-bench results do not show whether code is maintainable, secure, provably correct, or well-crafted; they show only that unit tests pass.
source_excerpt: The source states: "With this benchmark we do not have any idea if the code is maintainable, secure, provably correct, or well-crafted—we just know that the unit test cases for it will pass."
note: This is an explicit limit on what benchmark success establishes about software quality.
caveat: This is an explicit limit on what benchmark success establishes about software quality.

evidence_text: Aider Polyglot measures whether Aider can solve hard Exercism problems and make file edits that pass unit tests after at most one round of feedback.
source_excerpt: The source says Aider Polyglot measures: "If the coding agent (specifically, Aider) can solve hard-level Exercism problems and apply file edits that pass unit tests after at most one round of feedback."
note: This evidence describes the benchmark target and evaluation setting.
caveat: This evidence describes the benchmark target and evaluation setting.

evidence_text: Aider Polyglot covers 225 problems across six languages: C++, Go, Java, JavaScript, Python, and Rust.
source_excerpt: The source lists: "C++ 26, Go 39, Java 47, JavaScript 49, Python 34, Rust 30, Total 225."
note: This is a language-coverage and problem-count fact.
caveat: This is a language-coverage and problem-count fact.

evidence_text: LiveCodeBench measures Python competitive-programming performance under hidden test suites using a rolling fresh problem set.
source_excerpt: The source says SWE-bench measures "How well a coding agent can submit a patch for a real-world GitHub issue that passes the unit tests for that issue."
note: This evidence states the benchmark’s stated target, not whether that target matches broader software engineering work.
caveat: This evidence states the benchmark’s stated target, not whether that target matches broader software engineering work.
